sources.title: 3.4.4. Standards Applicable to All Downtown Base Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: Required use of ground floor areas For development located on principal streets and other specified areas identified in the D-W, D-BC, D-MU, D-AD, and D-FN Downtown districts, no less than 65 percent of the length of the ground floor street-facing facade shall be devoted to one or more of the following activities, and in the D-SP district, no less than 50 percent of the ground floor area shall be devoted to the following activities: Commercial establishments. Display windows and display rooms fo
sources.full_text: Required use of ground floor areas For development located on principal streets and other specified areas identified in the D-W, D-BC, D-MU, D-AD, and D-FN Downtown districts, no less than 65 percent of the length of the ground floor street-facing facade shall be devoted to one or more of the following activities, and in the D-SP district, no less than 50 percent of the ground floor area shall be devoted to the following activities: Commercial establishments. Display windows and display rooms for merchandise. Offices. Museums. Civic uses. Child and adult day care centers. Educational facilities. Rental offices (only in D-SP). Conference rooms (only in D-SP). Fenestration A minimum of 50 percent of the total area of ground floor facades in the D-W, D-BC, D-MU, D-AD, and D-FN Downtown districts shall be transparent. A minimum of 50 percent of those portions of ground floor façades of non-residential uses in the D-SP district shall be transparent. REVISED 2/26/2019 (Ordinance #47,528); REVISED 3/25/2025 (Ordinance #49,896)

sources.title: 3.4.5. D-W: Downtown - Waterfront
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Waterfront (D-W) district is to protect public access to the edge of the scenic Elizabeth River and views of the river from the rest of downtown Norfolk. Pedestrian circulation along the waterfront is encouraged by a waterfront walkway, which is intended to provide outdoor recreation opportunities, shoreline access and views, and connections to a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONA
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Waterfront (D-W) district is to protect public access to the edge of the scenic Elizabeth River and views of the river from the rest of downtown Norfolk. Pedestrian circulation along the waterfront is encouraged by a waterfront walkway, which is intended to provide outdoor recreation opportunities, shoreline access and views, and connections to a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS DowntownCharacter District Standard Residential Mixed-Use All Other Uses Lot Coverage, max. (% of site area) 60 Build-to line (% of building frontage abutting property line) 75 Open Space (% lot area) 10 Floor Area Ratio, max. (gross floor area / lot area) 3.0 3.0 4.0 District-specific Development STANDARDS Pedestrian Walkways Development fronting on the Elizabeth River shall provide access to the river through provision of a continuous and convenient pedestrian circulation area along the water's edge including a waterfront walkway, or esplanade, at least 10 feet in width, within a landscaped area at least 25 feet in width. Public access to the walkway or esplanade shall be provided through waterfront properties. Required Use of Ground Floor Areas Ground floor use requirements identified in Section 3.4.4.A, Required Use of Ground Floor Areas, shall apply to all buildings with facades oriented toward the waterfront pedestrian walkway required in subsection (1), above. Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

sources.title: 3.4.6. D-BC: Downtown - Business Center
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Business Center (D-BC) district is to recognize downtown Norfolk's role as the regional business, economic, and cultural center of Hampton Roads. The district provides lands that support a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses. The district is also intended to recognize historic buildings that give downtown Norfolk its distinctive character, such as the Customs House, St. Paul's Church, and the MacArthur Memo
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Business Center (D-BC) district is to recognize downtown Norfolk's role as the regional business, economic, and cultural center of Hampton Roads. The district provides lands that support a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses. The district is also intended to recognize historic buildings that give downtown Norfolk its distinctive character, such as the Customs House, St. Paul's Church, and the MacArthur Memorial. Development near such structures should contribute to their historic character. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard DowntownCharacter District Residential Mixed-Use All Other Uses Lot Coverage, max. (% of site area) 100 Build-to line (% of building frontage abutting property line) 75 Open Space (% lot area) 10 Floor Area Ratio, max. (gross floor area / lot area) 5.0 5.0 6.0 Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet District-specific Development STANDARDS Required Use of Ground Floor Areas Ground floor use requirements identified in Section 3.4.4.A, Required Use of Ground Floor Areas, shall apply to buildings oriented toward the following streets: Main Street; Plume Street; City Hall Avenue; Granby Street; Atlantic Street; Bank Street; Commercial Place; St. Paul’s Boulevard; Monticello Avenue; and Boush Street. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

sources.title: 3.4.7. D-MU: Downtown - Mixed-use
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown – Mixed-Use (D-MU) is to encourage the adaptive reuse of existing buildings and the creation of new infill structures at a scale that is conducive to pedestrian circulation and the creation of relatively compact developments. The district provides lands that support a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses. sources.title: 3.4.8. D-AD: Downtown - Arts and Design
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Arts and Design (D-AD) district is to recognize downtown Norfolk as the preeminent regional center for the visual, performing, and design arts. The district provides lands that support a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses, which are intended to support creativity, skill, and talent in fine arts, creative arts, advertising, architecture, antiques, culinary arts, crafts, design, fashion, live performance, mu
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Arts and Design (D-AD) district is to recognize downtown Norfolk as the preeminent regional center for the visual, performing, and design arts. The district provides lands that support a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses, which are intended to support creativity, skill, and talent in fine arts, creative arts, advertising, architecture, antiques, culinary arts, crafts, design, fashion, live performance, music, publishing, radio, and television. sources.title: 3.4.9. D-FN: Downtown - Fort Norfolk
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown – Fort Norfolk (D-FN) district is to promote the transition of the Fort Norfolk area, also known as Atlantic City, from a predominately heavy industrial area to a mixed-use waterfront setting that is more consistent with other downtown districts. The district provides lands for a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses, and is also intended to protect views of the Elizabeth River and create public access to the w
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown – Fort Norfolk (D-FN) district is to promote the transition of the Fort Norfolk area, also known as Atlantic City, from a predominately heavy industrial area to a mixed-use waterfront setting that is more consistent with other downtown districts. The district provides lands for a wide range of intensive multi-family, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses, and is also intended to protect views of the Elizabeth River and create public access to the waterfront. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard DowntownCharacter District Residential Mixed-Use All Other Uses Lot Coverage, max. (% of site area) 80 Build-to line (% of building frontage abutting property line) 75 Front Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 20 [1] Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) Buildings under 3 stories 15 [2] Buildings between three and five stories 20 [2] Buildings over five stories 25 [2] Corner Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) Buildings under 3 stories 15 [2] Buildings between three and five stories 20 [2] Buildings over five stories 25 [2] Building separation, (ft.) Buildings under 3 stories 15 [2][3] Buildings between three and five stories 20 [2][3] Buildings over five stories 25 [2][3] Open Space (% lot area) 10 10 10 Floor Area Ratio, max. (gross floor area / lot area) 3.5 4.0 2.0 Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] Only applies to buildings taller than 20 feet located on the north side of Front Street. [2] Only applies to properties located on the south side of Front Street. [3] Only applies to properties with multiple buildings. District-specific Development STANDARDS Pedestrian Walkways Development fronting on the Elizabeth River shall provide access to the river through provision of a continuous and convenient pedestrian circulation area along the water's edge including a waterfront walkway, or esplanade, at least 10 feet in width, within a landscaped area at least 25 feet in width. Public access to the walkway or esplanade shall be provided through waterfront properties Required Use of Ground Floor Areas Ground floor use requirements identified in Section 3.4.4.A, Required Use of Ground Floor Areas, shall apply to buildings oriented toward the north side of Front Street. Off-street parking and loading Two parking spaces shall be required per dwelling unit for all residential uses. All parking shall be located within 100 feet of the southern district boundary line along the Elizabeth River unless separated from the river by a public right-of-way. All loading and maneuvering space shall be concealed from view from streets, public open space, and pedestrian ways and shall be located within building lines. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

sources.title: 3.4.10. D-SP: Downtown - Saint Paul\u0027s
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Downtown - Saint Paul’s (D-SP) district is to encourage redevelopment at a scale that is conducive to pedestrian circulation and is connected and integrated into Downtown. The district provides lands that support a wide range of intensely developed multi-family residential, commercial, civic, institutional, and office uses. sources.title: 3.4.11. Uses for the Downtown Base Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: PURPOSE The purpose of this section is to authorize the establishment and continuation of land uses that are allowed on a parcel in a Downtown base zoning district. This section identifies the Downtown zoning districts in which such uses are allowed, identifies what type of permit or review is required to establish them, and provides reference to any special performance standards applicable to particular uses. sources.full_text: PURPOSE The purpose of this section is to authorize the establishment and continuation of land uses that are allowed on a parcel in a Downtown base zoning district. This section identifies the Downtown zoning districts in which such uses are allowed, identifies what type of permit or review is required to establish them, and provides reference to any special performance standards applicable to particular uses. USE TABLE FOR USES IN DOWNTOWN BASE DISTRICTS Table 3.4.11, Principal, Accessory, and Temporary Use Table for Downtown Base Zoning Districts, lists allowable uses and shows whether each use is permitted or prohibited within the various Downtown zoning districts, as well as the type of permit or development approval by which the use may be allowed. It further references any performance standards applicable to specific uses regardless of the zoning district in which they are allowed or the review procedure by which they are approved, unless expressly stated to the contrary. sources.title: 3.5.1. General Purposes of Industrial Base Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: The purpose and intent of the industrial base zoning districts are to: Create suitable environments for various types of industrial businesses, and protect them from the adverse effects of incompatible development; Strengthen the local economy and provide employment opportunities; Accommodate new infill development and redevelopment that is consistent with surrounding context and character; Ensure industrial development is located and designed to protect and preserve the character of existing si
sources.full_text: The purpose and intent of the industrial base zoning districts are to: Create suitable environments for various types of industrial businesses, and protect them from the adverse effects of incompatible development; Strengthen the local economy and provide employment opportunities; Accommodate new infill development and redevelopment that is consistent with surrounding context and character; Ensure industrial development is located and designed to protect and preserve the character of existing single-family districts and neighborhoods; Improve the design quality of industrial areas; and Help implement the comprehensive plan and other plans adopted by City Council.

sources.title: 3.5.2. Established Industrial Base Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: The industrial base zoning districts established by this Ordinance are identified in Table 3.5.2, Established Industrial Base Zoning Districts. Table 3.5.2. Established Industrial Base Zoning Districts I-L: Industrial - Light I-G: Industrial - General I-W: Industrial - Waterfront I-DW: Industrial - Deep Water (Industrial Districts Use Table)
sources.full_text: The industrial base zoning districts established by this Ordinance are identified in Table 3.5.2, Established Industrial Base Zoning Districts. Table 3.5.2. Established Industrial Base Zoning Districts I-L: Industrial - Light I-G: Industrial - General I-W: Industrial - Waterfront I-DW: Industrial - Deep Water (Industrial Districts Use Table)

sources.title: 3.5.3. I-L: Industrial - Light
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Industrial - Light (I-L) district is to provide lands to accommodate a range of primarily indoor industrial uses, such as wholesale, warehousing, distribution, light fabrication or processing, light repair and service, storage, and limited assembly uses. District standards are also intended to minimize impacts to adjacent residential land uses. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Downtown or Traditional Character District Suburban or Coastal Character District
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Industrial - Light (I-L) district is to provide lands to accommodate a range of primarily indoor industrial uses, such as wholesale, warehousing, distribution, light fabrication or processing, light repair and service, storage, and limited assembly uses. District standards are also intended to minimize impacts to adjacent residential land uses. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Downtown or Traditional Character District Suburban or Coastal Character District All Uses All Uses Lot Area, min. (sf.) 10,000 10,000 Lot Width, min. (ft.) 100 100 Front Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 10 [1] 10 Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 0 [2] 0 [2] Corner Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 10 [1] 10 Rear Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 0 [2] 0 [2] Building Height, max. (ft.) 65 [3] 65 [3] Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] Minimum front or corner side yard setback shall be equal to the average front or corner side yard setback for properties on the same block face or 10 feet, whichever is less. [2] A Type C buffer (see Section 5.3, Perimeter Buffers) is required on properties adjacent to residential districts. [3] Maximum building height of 35 ft. if adjacent to residential districts. REVISED 6/26/18 (Ordinance #47,268)

sources.title: 3.5.4. I-G: Industrial - General
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Industrial - General Light (I-G) district is to provide lands to accommodate a range of intensive indoor and outdoor industrial uses such as fabrication, manufacturing, assembly, processing and bulk storage. District standards are also intended to minimize impacts to adjacent residential land uses. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Downtown or Traditional Character District Suburban or Coastal Character District All Uses All Uses Lot Area, min. (sf.) 30,000

sources.title: 3.5.5. I-W: Industrial - Waterfront
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Industrial - Waterfront (I-W) district is to provide lands to accommodate water-dependent maritime industrial activities which do not require direct access to deep water channels. This includes marinas, yacht clubs, seafood processing, ship building and repair, and utility facilities. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard All Character Districts All Uses Lot Area, min. sources.title: 3.6.1. Purpose
sources.excerpt: The purpose of the Historic and Cultural Conservation base zoning districts is to: Protect and conserve areas of the city and individual structures, premises and sites having special historic or cultural significance; Encourage new development and redevelopment that is consistent with the character of existing development in the historic districts; Prevent destruction of historic structures and encroachment by uses, structures and site development features that are incompatible with the unique a
sources.full_text: The purpose of the Historic and Cultural Conservation base zoning districts is to: Protect and conserve areas of the city and individual structures, premises and sites having special historic or cultural significance; Encourage new development and redevelopment that is consistent with the character of existing development in the historic districts; Prevent destruction of historic structures and encroachment by uses, structures and site development features that are incompatible with the unique appearance and historic and cultural significance in the historic districts; and Implement the historic preservation policies in the adopted comprehensive plan.

sources.title: 3.6.3. Historic District Guidelines
sources.excerpt: Development in the historic and cultural conservation districts shall comply with the City of Norfolk Historic District Design Guidelines, which is incorporated herein by reference.
sources.full_text: Development in the historic and cultural conservation districts shall comply with the City of Norfolk Historic District Design Guidelines, which is incorporated herein by reference.

breadcrumb: 3.6 Historic and Cultural Conservation Base Zoning Districts

sources.title: 3.6.4. Standards Applicable to All Historic and Cultural Conservation Base Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: Fenestration Existing Commercial Buildings Any window facing a public street shall be kept transparent. Nothing shall be installed or positioned on the window to block the window's transparency (such as signage, films, fixtures, or furnishings). Interior or exterior decorative window treatments that do not obscure more than 50 percent of the required transparency are allowed. New Commercial Buildings A minimum of 50 percent of the total area of the ground floor of a façade facing a public street
sources.full_text: Fenestration Existing Commercial Buildings Any window facing a public street shall be kept transparent. Nothing shall be installed or positioned on the window to block the window's transparency (such as signage, films, fixtures, or furnishings). Interior or exterior decorative window treatments that do not obscure more than 50 percent of the required transparency are allowed. New Commercial Buildings A minimum of 50 percent of the total area of the ground floor of a façade facing a public street shall be transparent. Nothing shall be installed or positioned on a window to block the window's transparency (such as signage, films, fixtures, or furnishings). Interior or exterior decorative window treatments that do not obscure more than 50 percent of required transparency are allowed. Deviation from Height Requirements In the HC-G1, HC-G2, HC-G3, HC-WF1, and HC-WF2 districts, height requirements may be exceeded after first receiving approval of a Certificate of Appropriateness in accordance with Section 2.4.10, 2.4.10. Certificate of Appropriateness, and then approval of a Conditional Use Permit in accordance with Section 2.4.8, 2.4.8. Conditional Use Permit. Parking Requirements for Specific Districts Required Maintenance All buildings and structures within an Historic and Cultural Conservation base zoning district shall be maintained in good repair, kept in a structurally sound fashion, and be reasonably protected against decay and deterioration in compliance with the applicable maintenance provisions of the Uniform Statewide Building Code. REVISED 4/24/2018 (Ordinance #47,198)

breadcrumb: 3.6 Historic and Cultural Conservation Base Zoning Districts

sources.title: 3.6.5. HC-G1: Ghent Historic and Cultural Conservation
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Ghent Historic and Cultural Conservation (HC-G1) district is to protect the existing concentration of harmonious and noteworthy structures and sites in Ghent, which is one of the first planned residential suburbs in the city. District standards encourage uses and activities which foster the conservation and improvement of historic structures and sites, and prevent the creation or encroachment of adverse environmental influences. Allowed uses are primarily residential i
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Ghent Historic and Cultural Conservation (HC-G1) district is to protect the existing concentration of harmonious and noteworthy structures and sites in Ghent, which is one of the first planned residential suburbs in the city. District standards encourage uses and activities which foster the conservation and improvement of historic structures and sites, and prevent the creation or encroachment of adverse environmental influences. Allowed uses are primarily residential in order to ensure new structures are compatible with district character. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Traditional Character District Single Family Two-Family Townhouse Multi-Family All Other Uses Lot Area, min. (sf.) 2,500 3,750 1,800 1,650 [1] 10,000 Lot Width, min. (ft.) 25 37.5 18 50 100 Lot Coverage, max. (% of lot) 55 Front Yard Setback (ft.) 17-20 [2] Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 3 [3] Corner Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 5 [4] Rear Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 3 [3] Building Separation, min. (ft.) 3 Building Height (ft.) 25-35 Accessory Building Height, max. (ft.) 25 Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] This standard applies per dwelling unit. The total lot area must not be less than 5,000 square feet. [2] If at least 25 percent of the block face has a front yard depth that is less than the standard, the minimum front yard setback shall be within 1.5 feet of the block face average. [3] Does not apply to structures sharing common walls at lot lines where there is no space between buildings. [4] If the lot is less than 30 feet wide, the setback is 3 feet. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the East Freemason Historic and Cultural Conservation (HC-EF) district is to protect the existing concentration of harmonious and noteworthy structures and sites in East Freemason, which includes structures listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and also to promote downtown Norfolk as a cultural center for the region. District standards encourage uses and activities which foster the conservation and improvement of historic structures and sites, prevent the creation or encroachment of adverse environmental influences, and ensure new structures and uses are compatible with district character. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard DowntownCharacter District Residential Mixed-Use All Other Uses Lot Coverage, max. (% of lot) 55 55 25 Building Height, max. (ft.) Two-story building 50 Three-story building 50 [1] Four or more stories 60 [2] Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] The first-floor elevation of three-story buildings shall be between four and five feet above grade unless a greater elevation is required by applicable flood plain regulations. [2] The first-floor elevation of buildings with four or more stories may be at grade unless a greater elevation is required by applicable flood plain regulations. sources.title: 3.6.11. Uses for the Historic and Cultural Conservation Base Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: PURPOSE The purpose of this section is to authorize the establishment and continuation of land uses that are allowed on a parcel in a Historic and Cultural Conservation base zoning district. This section identifies the Historic and Cultural Conservation zoning districts in which such uses are allowed, identifies what type of permit or review is required to establish them, and provides reference to any special performance standards applicable to particular uses. USE TABLE FOR USES IN HISTORIC AND
sources.full_text: PURPOSE The purpose of this section is to authorize the establishment and continuation of land uses that are allowed on a parcel in a Historic and Cultural Conservation base zoning district. This section identifies the Historic and Cultural Conservation zoning districts in which such uses are allowed, identifies what type of permit or review is required to establish them, and provides reference to any special performance standards applicable to particular uses. USE TABLE FOR USES IN HISTORIC AND CULTURAL CONSERVATION BASE DISTRICTS Table 3.6.11, Principal, Accessory, and Temporary Use Table for Historic and Cultural Conservation Base Zoning Districts, lists allowable uses and shows whether each use is permitted or prohibited within the various Historic and Cultural Conservation zoning districts, as well as the type of permit or development approval by which the use may be allowed. It further references any performance standards applicable to specific uses regardless of the zoning district in which they are allowed or the review procedure by which they are approved, unless expressly stated to the contrary. breadcrumb: 3.6 Historic and Cultural Conservation Base Zoning Districts

sources.title: 3.7.1. General Purpose of Special Purpose Districts
sources.excerpt: The purpose and intent of Special Purpose zoning districts are to: Accommodate development patterns that do not fit within other base zoning classifications (e.g., residential, commercial, industrial) due to unique physical characteristics or land use characteristics; Allow for the development, maintenance and expansion of special land uses, such as airports or medical centers that have unique character and needs; and Address land uses which are otherwise difficult to regulate with other base di
sources.full_text: The purpose and intent of Special Purpose zoning districts are to: Accommodate development patterns that do not fit within other base zoning classifications (e.g., residential, commercial, industrial) due to unique physical characteristics or land use characteristics; Allow for the development, maintenance and expansion of special land uses, such as airports or medical centers that have unique character and needs; and Address land uses which are otherwise difficult to regulate with other base district standards.

sources.title: 3.7.3. IN: Institutional
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Institutional (IN) district is to provide lands that accommodate institutional land uses, with a variety of different, but related land uses, preferably in a campus-style environment. District standards promote the compatibility of uses while minimizing potential impacts on neighboring development. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard All Character Districts All Uses Lot Area, min. (acres) 1 Front Yard Setback, min(ft.) Adjacent to residential zoning district 2
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Institutional (IN) district is to provide lands that accommodate institutional land uses, with a variety of different, but related land uses, preferably in a campus-style environment. District standards promote the compatibility of uses while minimizing potential impacts on neighboring development. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard All Character Districts All Uses Lot Area, min. (acres) 1 Front Yard Setback, min(ft.) Adjacent to residential zoning district 20 [1] [2] Adjacent to non-residential zoning district 10 [1] [2] Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) [3] Corner Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) 5 Rear Yard Setback, min. (ft.) [4] Building Height, max. (ft.) [5] Landscaped Open Space (% lot area) 20 Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] If the property abuts another zoning district on the same block face, the front yard setback shall be the same that is required in the abutting district. [2] If the property abuts two different zoning districts on the same block face, the front yard setback shall be the average required front yard setback for the two districts. [3] If the side yard abuts a different zoning district, the side yard setback shall be the same that is required in the abutting district, however for accessory buildings abutting a residential district, the side yard setback shall be 25 feet. [4] If the rear yard abuts a different zoning district, the rear yard setback shall be the same that is required in the abutting district. If abutting a residential zoning district, a five-foot buffer yard is required, and the setback for accessory structures shall be 25 feet. [5] There is no maximum height limit, however building features on properties abutting a residential district shall not be taller than the horizontal distance between the building feature and boundary of the residential district. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

sources.title: 3.7.4. IN-C: Institutional - Campus
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Institutional - Campus (IN-C) district is to provide lands that accommodate multi-functional institutional uses, such as university and medical campuses. Development may include the grouping of multiple institutional buildings on large sites, and inter-related public, private, and nonprofit development. District standards are intended to provide adequate parking and pedestrian circulation; ensure infrastructure is adequately provided; mitigate traffic congestion; prote
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Institutional - Campus (IN-C) district is to provide lands that accommodate multi-functional institutional uses, such as university and medical campuses. Development may include the grouping of multiple institutional buildings on large sites, and inter-related public, private, and nonprofit development. District standards are intended to provide adequate parking and pedestrian circulation; ensure infrastructure is adequately provided; mitigate traffic congestion; protect surrounding residential and historic lands against incompatible uses, and facilitate the creation of an attractive and harmonious fabric of buildings, streets, and open spaces. Intensity and Dimensional Standards Standard All Character Districts All Uses Lot Area, min. (acres) 10 Front Yard Setback, min (ft.) External street frontage 20 Internal street frontage 10 Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) [1] Corner Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) External street frontage 20 Internal street frontage 10 Rear Yard Setback, min. (ft.) [1] Building Height, max. (ft.) [2] All Other Standards [3] Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] Setback not required, however for properties abutting a residential district a 10-foot buffer yard is required, and the setback for accessory structures in side or rear yards abutting residential districts is 25 feet. [2] There is no maximum height limit, however building features on properties abutting a residential district shall not be taller than the horizontal distance between the building feature and boundary of the residential district. [3] Consistent with approved Development Certificate as described in Section 3.7.4.C(1), Development Certificate Required. District-Specific Review Procedures Development Certificate Required A Development Certificate (see Section 2.4.9, Development Certificate) shall be obtained prior to the issuance of any Zoning Certificate for new principal structures and substantial improvements or additions to existing principal structures in the IN-C district. Review Standards A Development Certificate approved for the IN-C district shall comply with standards identified below. Proposed development shall be compatible with existing development within, and adjacent to, the IN-C district. Off-street parking and loading facilities shall be sufficient and provide for adequate circulation between existing and proposed buildings. Anticipated traffic generation and circulation patterns shall be compatible with internal and external street capacities and configurations. Proposed open space and landscaping areas shall be consistent with the purpose of the district, sufficiently located to satisfy the needs of the proposed development, and properly integrated with existing and proposed buildings, pedestrian ways and streets. Proposed pedestrian circulation within the district shall be properly integrated with buildings, parking areas, and open space. The form and function of proposed buildings shall be compatible with other buildings within and immediately adjacent to the district, in terms of building separation, massing, scale, and proportion. View corridors and historic buildings or structures both within and immediately adjacent to the district shall be sufficiently protected. Natural features and environmental quality shall be sufficiently protected and maintained. Potential noise shall be sufficiently mitigated. Reference to Other Development Standards Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities REVISED 6/11/19 (Ordinance #47,652)

sources.title: 3.7.5. A: Airport
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Airport (A) district is to provide lands to accommodate the efficient layout and operation of Norfolk International Airport, including facilities for commercial airline operations, air cargo, and general aviation. The district also allows a range of airport-serving retail and service uses. District standards support the implementation of the Norfolk International Airport Master Plan. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Suburban Character District All Uses All
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Airport (A) district is to provide lands to accommodate the efficient layout and operation of Norfolk International Airport, including facilities for commercial airline operations, air cargo, and general aviation. The district also allows a range of airport-serving retail and service uses. District standards support the implementation of the Norfolk International Airport Master Plan. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Suburban Character District All Uses All Dimensional and Intensity Standards [1] Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] Consistent with approved Airport Master Plan as described in Section 3.7.5.C(1), Airport Master Plan. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC REVIEW PROCEDURES Airport Master Plan General Prior to seeking a Zoning Certificate in the A district, the land owner shall prepare, and have approved in accordance with this section an Airport Master Plan, which demonstrates how development, land uses, and airport-related functions will be coordinated in the district. Review of Airport Master Plan The Airport Master Plan shall be reviewed by the Planning Commission and approved by City Council. Amendments to Approved Airport Master Plan An Airport Master Plan shall be amended in accordance with the procedures and standards for its original approval. Effect Development in the A district shall be in substantial compliance with the approved Airport Master Plan, in addition to all other applicable standards and requirements of this Ordinance. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC DEVELOPMENT STANDARDS Existing Landscaping The wooded and landscaped character of the approach roads, parking facilities, and terminal building shall be retained. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

sources.title: 3.7.6. OSP: Open Space and Preservation
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Open Space and Preservation (OSP) district is to provide lands to accommodate the preservation and protection of active park and recreation activities, passive open space lands, significant natural features, and environmentally sensitive lands. District standards limit development in these areas in order to preserve the city’s natural, scenic, and recreational assets; ensure their proper functioning; and promote visitor enjoyment. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS St
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Open Space and Preservation (OSP) district is to provide lands to accommodate the preservation and protection of active park and recreation activities, passive open space lands, significant natural features, and environmentally sensitive lands. District standards limit development in these areas in order to preserve the city’s natural, scenic, and recreational assets; ensure their proper functioning; and promote visitor enjoyment. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard All Character Districts All Uses All Dimensional and Intensity Standards [1] Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] There are no specific intensity and dimensional standards for the OSP district. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC REVIEW PROCEDURES Development in the OSP district shall be: Consistent with OSP district purposes; and Appropriately mitigated to ensure development does not adversely affect the natural features and functions of district lands. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities

District standards limit development to preserve the natural and scenic assets of the district and ensure the proper
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Resource Conservation (RESOURCE) district is to provide lands to accommodate the preservation and protection of natural resources. Lands in this district are primarily owned by the city and the Norfolk Redevelopment and Housing Authority, and are intended for passive recreation uses, and the preservation of open space and environmentally-sensitive lands. sources.title: 3.7.8. MI: Military Installation
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Military Installation (MI) district is to recognize and support major single-use and multiple-use military facilities in the city that are owned and operated by the federal military. While federal military lands are not subject to this Ordinance and other local ordinances and codes, they occupy large land areas in the city and are therefore identified on the Official Zoning Map to differentiate them from lands that are subject to the Zoning Ordinance. INTENSITY AND DIM
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Military Installation (MI) district is to recognize and support major single-use and multiple-use military facilities in the city that are owned and operated by the federal military. While federal military lands are not subject to this Ordinance and other local ordinances and codes, they occupy large land areas in the city and are therefore identified on the Official Zoning Map to differentiate them from lands that are subject to the Zoning Ordinance. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard All Character Districts All Uses All Dimensional and Intensity Standards [1] Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] There are no specific intensity and dimensional standards for the MI district. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC REVIEW PROCEDURES Development Coordination General The military is encouraged to establish a coordinated planning process with the city when developing lands within the MI district. Goals for Development Coordination The purpose of coordinating development activities is to: Achieve mutual benefits for the military and the city; Minimize undesirable development impacts; and Help meet the policies and objectives of the comprehensive plan. Development the city is Most Interested in Coordinating with the Military The city is particularly interested in coordinating the following types of development within the MI district with the military: Commercial; Residential; and Mixed-use.

sources.title: 3.7.9. MW: Monarch Way
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Monarch Way (MW) district is to provide lands to accommodate the development proposed in the Redevelopment Plan for the Hampton Boulevard Redevelopment Project, and accommodate the needs of Old Dominion University (a strategic institutional asset for the city and region). Allowed uses include a range of university-related institutional uses, a diverse type of residential uses, and campus and neighborhood-serving commercial and office uses. District standards are intend
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Monarch Way (MW) district is to provide lands to accommodate the development proposed in the Redevelopment Plan for the Hampton Boulevard Redevelopment Project, and accommodate the needs of Old Dominion University (a strategic institutional asset for the city and region). Allowed uses include a range of university-related institutional uses, a diverse type of residential uses, and campus and neighborhood-serving commercial and office uses. District standards are intended to accommodate the growth of Old Dominion University and provide for orderly redevelopment of the surrounding area. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Traditional Character District All Uses Setback, max. (ft.) On Monarch Way 8 On a numbered street 15 On Hampton Boulevard 40 Setback, min. (ft.) On Killam Avenue 15 Building Height, max. (ft.) 75 Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet District-Specific Review Procedures A Development Certificate (see Section 2.4.9, Development Certificate) shall be obtained prior to the issuance of any Zoning Certificate for development in the Monarch Way district. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC Development Standards Development in the MW district shall comply with the following standards. Pedestrian Access Designated pedestrian pathways shall be provided and marked from the public sidewalk to the main entrances of the principal building. Mid-block plazas with landscaping shall be provided for residential development. Plazas shall include a paved, eight-foot-wide path between Monarch Way and the parking facilities. Development that is required to provide 50 or more parking spaces shall provide pedestrian access between the parking facilities and major building entrances. Parking facilities for residential development shall incorporate the following pedestrian features: A continuous sidewalk not less than four feet wide between the parking lot and the residential building. All parking spaces adjacent to the sidewalk shall have bumper logs to maintain a clear sidewalk; and A space not less than eight feet in width at each entrance to residential buildings to ensure unobstructed access to the sidewalk from the parking lot. This area shall be clearly marked and delineated. Façade Treatment A minimum of fifty percent of the total area of ground floor facades (as measured from a point beginning no higher than three feet above sidewalk grade, and ending at a point no higher than ten feet above sidewalk grade) along Monarch Way, Killam Avenue, and Hampton Boulevard shall be transparent from the street, in order to promote pedestrian activity and safety. Clear glass shall be used to maintain the transparency standard. No film shall be applied to the windows. At least one pedestrian entrance shall be provided for each retail space, academic space, and office space located along a facade fronting a public right-of-way. For multi-family residential dwellings, one pedestrian entrance shall be provided for each ground-level unit and lobby entrance. Signs The sign standards for commercial uses in Section 5.7, Signs, shall apply, subject to the following limitations: Freestanding signs are not permitted for ground floor, nonresidential uses. No more than two freestanding signs are permitted to identify each residential complex. The sign(s) shall be a monument type sign not to exceed 32 square feet of sign surface area per sign face. Maximum height of the sign shall be six feet. The sign(s) shall be landscaped three feet in depth from each side of the sign base. Wall signs, which are geometric in form, plastic coated, internally illuminated, and have a changeable face for each tenant are not permitted. Subject to a permit issued by the City of Norfolk, one A-frame sign on the public sidewalk in front of each individual storefront is permitted. The size, placement, removal, and other characteristics of A-frame signs shall be in accordance with the guidelines for A-frame signs. Review Standards Development shall be shall be consistent with: The comprehensive plan and other relevant, adopted plans; and The Monarch Way Design and Development Guidelines, as submitted by the Old Dominion Real Estate Foundation and approved by the Planning Commission. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities REVISED 10/25/2022 (Ordinance #48,962)

sources.title: 3.7.10. EBH: East Beach Harbor
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the East Beach Harbor (EBH) district is to provide lands to accommodate the district’s maritime-oriented character. Allowed uses include a diversity of residential uses and limited neighborhood-serving commercial uses that protect water views north of Pretty Lake Avenue. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Townhouse Coastal Character District Multi-Family All Other Uses Lot Area, min. (sf.) 2,000 N/A N/A Lot Width, min. (ft.) 20 N/A N/A Density, max. Dwelling unit
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the East Beach Harbor (EBH) district is to provide lands to accommodate the district’s maritime-oriented character. Allowed uses include a diversity of residential uses and limited neighborhood-serving commercial uses that protect water views north of Pretty Lake Avenue. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Townhouse Coastal Character District Multi-Family All Other Uses Lot Area, min. (sf.) 2,000 N/A N/A Lot Width, min. (ft.) 20 N/A N/A Density, max. Dwelling units per acre 36 36 36 Dwelling units per 1,000 sf of residential net building area 1 1 1 Front Yard Setback, (ft.) Minimum 0 [1] 0 [1] 0 [1] Maximum 7.5 [1] 7.5 [1] 7.5 [1] Side Yard Setback, min. (ft.) Adjacent to buildings with two or fewer floors 15 [1] 15 [1] 15 [1] Adjacent to buildings with three or four floor 20 [1] 20 [1] 20 [1] Adjacent to buildings with five floors 25 [1] 25 [1] 25 [1] Rear Yard Setback, min. (ft.) [1] [1] [1] Building Length, max. (ft.) 250 [2] 250 [2] 250 [2] Building Height, max. (ft.) 35 [3] [3] Usable Open Space, min 100 sf/ dwelling [4] 100 sf/ dwelling [4] 20% of net lot area [4] Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] For lots adjacent to wetlands or water bodies, the setback shall be at least 15 feet from either the landward boundary of the wetland, or the mean high water level. [2] Only applies to structures greater than four stories in height. [3] Maximum building height shall be five stories. [4] For purposes of calculating usable open space, 100% of recreation areas and yards (excluding parking areas) may be counted toward the requirement; 50% of lawns, green spaces, and wetlands may be counted; and 25% of stormwater and utility easements may be counted. All facilities (including irregularly-shaped portions) must be at least 10 feet wide to count toward the usable open space requirement. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC REVIEW PROCEDURES Development Certificate Required A Development Certificate (see Section 2.4.9, Development Certificate) shall be obtained prior to the issuance of any Zoning Certificate for new principal structures and substantial improvements or additions to existing principal structures in the EBH district. District-Specific Development STANDARDS Development in the EBH district shall comply with the following standards. Location and Appearance of Required Off-Street Parking Parking shall not be allowed in required front yards, or yards adjacent to wetlands or water bodies. Parking shall not be directly visible from Pretty Lake Avenue. This can be mitigated by: Situating parking facilities behind or adjacent to buildings or structures; or Installing landscaping, decorative fences, or low walls. Building Design Except for townhouses: Articulation All exterior walls of buildings facing Pretty Lake Avenue or facing yards adjacent to wetlands or water bodies shall incorporate variations in the façade and roofline, such as: Recesses and projections of at least three feet; Variation in building materials and color; or Fenestration at intervals between 30 and 50 feet. Pedestrian Access Individual units on the ground floor shall have at least one primary pedestrian entrance opening to the street. Additional entrances may be located in side yards, including yards adjacent to wetlands and water bodies. Fenestration At least 50 percent of the ground floor façade facing Pretty Lake Avenue shall be transparent. At least 40 percent of upper floor façades facing Pretty Lake Avenue, and upper-floor facades facing wetlands and water bodies, shall be transparent. Materials No exterior portion of any building shall consist of unfinished or unpainted concrete. Ground Floor Treatment Height For all buildings greater than three stories in height, the ground floor shall be at least 33 percent taller than the upper stories, and no less than 12 feet in height. Arrangement For all lands adjacent to Pretty Lake Avenue, the ground floor of buildings facing Pretty Lake Avenue shall be designed and arranged to accommodate retail and office uses. Top Floor Treatment For all five-story buildings, the top story shall have a visual feature distinguishing the top of the building, such as: A course, decorative line; A change in finish material; or A change in window pattern. Projections Into Yards The following building features shall be permitted to project into certain yards as indicated: Projections from upper stories of a building may extend into the street yard up to the full extent of the yard. Projections from ground floors of buildings may extend into one-half of the depth of a street yard except for patios serving commercial establishments which may project up to the full extent of the yard. Projections from both ground floors and upper stories of buildings may extend into any required side yard or street yard to a maximum extent of 7½ feet. Projections that extend into any yard adjacent to water or wetlands shall only be from upper stories of buildings. Signs The only signs permitted in the district shall be wall signs that comply with the following regulations. All other standards and requirements set forth in Section 5.7, Signs, shall apply. All signs shall generally complement the architecture, materials, and colors of the building to which they are affixed. Size for signs shall be limited as follows: If mounted on the exterior wall most nearly parallel to and closest to Pretty Lake Avenue, the total cumulative area of signage may have a maximum of one square foot of sign surface area for each linear foot of the wall measured at the foundation. Signs placed on all other façades shall have a maximum cumulative size of one-half square foot of sign surface area for each linear foot of the building façade on the exposure involved. Signs may include only the following elements: Individual articulated letters. Painted signs (on the building or on other suitable surface). Neon tubing. Lettering on an awning. Signs extending perpendicular to the front façade of the building (blade signs). Box wall signs, which typically are geometric in form, plastic coated, internally lighted, and have a changeable face, are not permitted. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities REVISED 6/11/19 (Ordinance #47,652)

sources.title: 3.7.11. TOD: Transit-Oriented Development
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) district is to encourage the location of uses that will enhance the street-level experience while providing for a mix of transit-supportive uses within no more than ½ mile of a fixed transit station. Applicability General Applicability The standards and requirements of this section apply to individually-designated TOD districts. sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) district is to encourage the location of uses that will enhance the street-level experience while providing for a mix of transit-supportive uses within no more than ½ mile of a fixed transit station. Applicability General Applicability The standards and requirements of this section apply to individually-designated TOD districts. Specific Areas of Applicability Two TOD districts are established by the city. Those established include: The Transit-Oriented Development Core (TOD-C) District (see Section 3.7.11.F(1)); and The Transit-Oriented Development Support (TOD-S) District (see Section 3.7.11.F(2)). District Boundaries Individual TOD districts shall be represented on the Official Zoning Map. PROCEDURE AND CRITERIA FOR DESIGNATION Individual TOD districts shall be established as a Zoning Map Amendment consistent with Section 2.4.3, Zoning Map Amendment. REVIEW PROCEDURES APPLICABLE TO TOD DISTRICTS A Development Certificate (see Section 2.4.9, Development Certificate) shall be obtained prior to the issuance of any Zoning Certificate for new principal structures and substantial improvements or additions to existing principal structures in the TOD district. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC DEVELOPMENT STANDARDS Form Standards Notwithstanding those standards listed below, any development in the TOD districts shall be considered in a Traditional Character District for the purposes of Section 5.9, Form Standards. All buildings shall provide a clearly distinguishable primary façade including a principal entrance into the building. Buildings shall be oriented toward the street. If a rail transit station is located within 250 feet of the building, the building may be oriented toward the street or rail transit station. For property with frontage on multiple streets, the ZA shall determine which street buildings shall be oriented toward. The first 12 feet above grade of the primary façade of non-residential and mixed-use buildings shall include a minimum of 50 percent transparent area. No more than 30 percent of the transparent area may be covered by window signs. Location of Parking Facilities Surface parking lots shall be located behind buildings except where space behind buildings is not entirely adequate, in which case that parking that cannot be accommodated behind buildings may be located beside a building. No parking shall be located between a building and any right-of-way. In addition to the landscape planting requirements of Article 5, surface parking lots shall be screened along all streets by a masonry wall or fence four feet in height. Parking structures that abut a street shall have at least 50 percent of the ground floor fronting on any street developed with office, retail, or other pedestrian-oriented uses. Wherever single-family dwellings are permitted, the following regulations shall apply: Attached and detached garages shall be set back a minimum of ten feet behind the primary building façade. Garages shall not make up more than 50 percent of the overall width of the dwelling. Off-street Loading For all buildings located within 250 feet of a transit station, off-street loading is not permitted in any location visible from the right-of-way along which the primary building façade is located. Signs The sign standards of Section 5.7, Signs, applicable to Downtown Districts shall apply, except that no pole signs are permitted. SPECIFIC TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT DISTRICTS The following TOD districts have been established: Transit-Oriented Development Core (TOD-C) Purpose Statement The purpose of the Transit-Oriented Development Core (TOD-C) district is to promote transit-, bicycle-, and pedestrian-oriented uses while discouraging low-intensity, auto-oriented uses. Eligibility for Rezoning At the time of the creation of this district, no properties are zoned or rezoned to lie within the district. To be included in the district, a Zoning Map Amendment must be approved. No property is eligible to be rezoned to this district except those which meet the following criteria: Only properties located within ¼ mile of a fixed-rail transit station shall be eligible for designation as TOD-C. No property located in any HC district shall be eligible for designation as TOD-C. Development Standards Intensity and Dimensional Standards The intensity and dimensional standards found in Table 3.7.11(A), Intensity and Dimensional Standards – TOD-C District, shall apply to all new development within the TOD-C district: TABLE 3.7.11(A): INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS – TOD-C DISTRICT Standard Single-Family Detached, Single-Family Semi-Detached, Two-Family, Townhouse Multi-Family Non-Residential and Mixed-Use Lot Coverage (% of lot area) Minimum Use Standards of MF-NS (see Section 3.2.9) 75 75 Maximum 85 100 Build-to Line (min. % of primary building façade abutting property line) 75 75 Open Space, min. (% lot area) 25-35 25-35 Building Height (ft.) Minimum 30 30 Maximum 80 80 Parking Requirements The minimum number of parking spaces required shall equal 50 percent of the minimum required in Table 5.1.7(D), Minimum Number of Parking Spaces. Transit-Oriented Development Support (TOD-S) Purpose Statement The purpose of the Transit-Oriented Development Support (TOD-S) district is to facilitate the harmonious transition between a TOD-C district and the surrounding neighborhoods. Eligibility for Rezoning At the time of the creation of this district, no properties are zoned or rezoned to lie within the district. To be included in the district, a Zoning Map Amendment must be approved. No property is eligible to be rezoned to this district except those which meet the following criteria: Only properties located within ½ mile of a fixed-rail transit station shall be eligible for designation as TOD-S. No property located in any HC district shall be eligible for designation as TOD-S. Development Standards Intensity and Dimensional Standards The intensity and dimensional standards found in Table 3.7.11(B), Intensity and Dimensional Standards – TOD-S District, shall apply to all new development within the TOD-S district: TABLE 3.7.11(B): INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS – TOD-S DISTRICT Standard Single-Family Detached, Single-Family Semi-Detached, Two-Family, Townhouse Multi-Family Non-Residential and Mixed-Use Lot Coverage (% of lot area) Minimum Use Standards of MF-NS (see Section 3.2.9) 65 65 Maximum 75 85 Build-to Line (min. % of primary building façade abutting property line) 75 75 Open Space, min. (% lot area) 25-35 25-35 Building Height (ft.) Minimum 30 30 Maximum 45 45 Parking Requirements The minimum number of parking spaces required shall equal 65 percent of the minimum required in Table 5.1.7(D), Minimum Number of Parking Spaces. REVISED 11/20/18 (Ordinance #47,439); REVISED 6/11/19 (Ordinance #47,652)

sources.title: 3.7.12. G-1: Granby/Monticello Corridor Mixed-use
sources.excerpt: Purpose The purpose of the Granby/Monticello Corridor Mixed-Use (G-1) district is to support the transition of lands in the district from a predominately industrial and automobile-oriented character to one that is compatible with the residential and institutional character of Ghent, which is adjacent to the G-1 district. Allowed uses include a variety of high-intensity and pedestrian-oriented residential and mixed-use development. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Traditional Characte
sources.full_text: Purpose The purpose of the Granby/Monticello Corridor Mixed-Use (G-1) district is to support the transition of lands in the district from a predominately industrial and automobile-oriented character to one that is compatible with the residential and institutional character of Ghent, which is adjacent to the G-1 district. Allowed uses include a variety of high-intensity and pedestrian-oriented residential and mixed-use development. INTENSITY AND DIMENSIONAL STANDARDS Standard Traditional Character District Residential Mixed-Use All Other Uses Lot Coverage, max. (% of lot area) 80 80 80 Front Yard Setback (max.) Frontage on a principal street [1] 10 [2] 10 [2] 10 [2] Frontage on all other streets 10 [3] 10 [3] 10 [3] Usable Open Space, min. (% of lot area) 15 15 15 Building Height, max. (ft.) 65 85 65 Notes: sf. = square feet; ft. = feet [1] Principal streets include Granby Street, Monticello Avenue, Princess Anne Road, and 21st Street. [2] At least 90 percent of the building façade needs to meet this standard. [3] At least 65 percent of the building façade needs to meet this standard. DISTRICT-SPECIFIC REVIEW PROCEDURES Development Certificate Required A Development Certificate (see Section 2.4.9, Development Certificate) shall be obtained prior to the issuance of any Zoning Certificate for new principal structures and substantial improvements or additions to existing principal structures in the G-1 district. District-Specific Development STANDARDS Development in the G-1 district shall comply with the following standards. Required Use of Ground Floor Areas Development with frontage on Granby Street, Monticello Avenue, Princess Anne Road, and 21st Street shall dedicate a minimum of 65 percent of the ground floor to: Commercial or office uses; Retail sales and service uses that include display windows; Child or adult day care uses; or Residential dwellings that provide an exterior patio, stoop, or usable balcony. At least 50 percent of the front façade shall be transparent from the street for uses identified under subsection (a), above. Appearance of Off-street Parking and Loading Facilities Loading Areas All loading areas shall be: Concealed from view, to the extent practical, from: Granby Street; Monticello Avenue; Princess Anne Road; 21st Street; Public open spaces; and Pedestrian ways. Located behind the front façade of the principal building. Off-street Parking Facilities A maximum of 25 percent of the length of the ground floor façade may consist of parking facilities visible from the public right-of-way, on: Granby Street; Monticello Avenue; Princess Anne Road; or 21st Street. Reference to other Development STANDARDS Article 1 Administration 1.1 Signs Article 1 Performance Standards 1.1 Exterior Lighting 1.1 Parking, Loading, and Bicycle Standards 1.1 Form Standards 1.1 Landscaping Standards 1.1 Neighborhood Protection 1.1 Perimeter Buffers 1.1 Accessory Structures 1.1 Screening 1.1 Resilience Quotient 1.1 Open Space Set-Asides Article 1 Nonconformities REVISED 6/11/2019 (Ordinance #47,652); REVISED 3/23/2021 (Ordinance #48,326)

sources.title: 3.7.13. Uses for the Special Purpose Districts
sources.excerpt: PURPOSE The purpose of this section is to authorize the establishment and continuation of land uses that are allowed on a parcel in a Special Purpose base zoning district. This section identifies the Special Purpose zoning districts in which such uses are allowed, identifies what type of permit or review is required to establish them, and provides reference to any special performance standards applicable to particular uses. USE TABLE FOR USES IN HISTORIC AND CULTURAL CONSERVATION BASE DISTRICTS

sources.title: 3.8.1. Established Planned Development Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: The PD: Planned Development zoning district is established by this Ordinance.
sources.full_text: The PD: Planned Development zoning district is established by this Ordinance.

sources.title: 3.8.2. General Provisions for Planned Development Zoning Districts
sources.excerpt: General Purposes of Planned Development Zoning Districts The purpose and intent of Planned Development (PD) zoning districts is to encourage innovative and efficient land planning and physical design concepts on sites at least three acres in area or greater.
